name: Attribute-Based Evidence Accumulation (Drift-Diffusion)
description: Dynamic stochastic decision process where value evidence for each candidate action accumulates over internal deliberation time steps. Drift rates are determined by attribute-weighted option values (immediate proximity attribute + abstract palatability attribute), and noise is scaled by attribute conflict (disagreement between attribute dimensions). Actions are selected when an accumulator first crosses a decision threshold, or by highest accumulated evidence at timeout. This produces naturalistic choice stochasticity and captures the prediction from Rangel (2013) that attribute conflict leads to slower, noisier decisions. Attribute weights are modulated by hunger and updated via reward prediction error. Grounded in postulates P1 (weighted summation), P3 (weight modulation), and Rangel (2013) prediction on conflict and reaction times.

variables.name: evidence_accumulator
variables.description: Running total of value evidence favoring action a at deliberation step t. One accumulator per candidate action. Reset to 0 at the start of each simulation step's deliberation.

variables.name: drift_rate
variables.description: Mean rate of evidence accumulation for action a, derived from the attribute-weighted value of the option targeted by that action.

variables.name: immediate_attribute_value
variables.description: Proximity-based desirability of the resource targeted by the action. For eat: 1.0 if food at position. For movement: 1.0 - manhattan_distance / max_distance to nearest food from next cell. For stay: 0.0.

variables.name: abstract_attribute_value
variables.description: Quality/palatability attribute of the targeted resource. Derived from food palatability: a_abs = 2 * (palatability - 0.1) / 0.9 - 1. Range [-1, 1].

variables.name: immediate_weight
variables.description: Weight for immediate attribute, dynamically set by hunger state. Computed via functional form ensuring w_imm + w_abs = 1.

variables.name: abstract_weight
variables.description: Weight for abstract attribute. Complement of w_imm.

variables.name: hunger
variables.description: Internal hunger drive. Rises by eta each step, drops by R_food when food is consumed. 0=sated, 1=maximally hungry.

variables.name: noise_magnitude
variables.description: Standard deviation of Gaussian noise in the evidence accumulation for action a, scaled by base noise and attribute conflict.

variables.name: attribute_conflict
variables.description: Degree of disagreement between immediate and abstract attribute values for the option targeted by action a. Higher conflict -> more noise -> more stochastic and slower decisions.

parameters.name: decision_threshold
parameters.source: Standard DDM calibration; agent commits when E_a >= theta

parameters.name: base_noise
parameters.source: Typical DDM noise level for moderate stochasticity

parameters.name: conflict_noise_scaling
parameters.source: Rangel (2013): attribute conflict increases RT and noise

parameters.name: weight_learning_rate
parameters.source: Standard reinforcement learning rate for attribute weight updates

rules.description: Compute attribute weights as a function of hunger. Hunger monotonically increases the immediate-attribute weight. No attentional learning in this formulation — weights are purely state-driven.
rules.pseudocode:
w_imm = (1 + H_t) / (2 + H_t)
w_abs = 1.0 / (2 + H_t)
# w_imm + w_abs = 1 always

rules.description: Compute attribute values for each candidate action, mapping food proximity to immediate attribute and food palatability to abstract attribute.
Compute drift rate for each action as the weighted linear sum of its attribute values.
Compute attribute conflict as absolute difference between immediate and abstract attribute values for the option targeted by each action.
rules.source_postulate: Rangel (2013) prediction: conflict -> slower, noisier choices

rules.description: Compute noise magnitude for each action, scaled by base noise and conflict.
rules.source_postulate: Rangel (2013): conflict increases decision noise and reaction time

rules.description: Evidence accumulation loop (discrete-time DDM). Each action has an independent accumulator starting at 0. At each deliberation step, drift + Gaussian noise is added. First accumulator to cross threshold wins.
rules.source_postulate: P2, Rangel (2013) prediction on conflict-RT relationship

rules.description: Attribute weight update via reward prediction error after action execution. Weights shift toward attributes that correlate with positive surprise, then renormalized.
rules.pseudocode:
RPE = reward - mu_selected
w_imm = w_imm + beta_w * RPE * a_imm_selected
w_abs = w_abs + beta_w * RPE * a_abs_selected
w_imm = max(0.05, w_imm)
w_abs = max(0.05, w_abs)
total = w_imm + w_abs
w_imm = w_imm / total
w_abs = w_abs / total

rules.description: Hunger state update. Rises each step, drops when food is consumed.
rules.pseudocode:
ate = 1 if (action == 'eat' and consumed) else 0
H_t = clip(H_t + eta - R_food * ate, 0, 1)

decision_logic.description: Each simulation step: (1) Read perception for position, food locations, hunger. (2) Compute hunger-driven attribute weights via R1. (3) For each of 6 actions, compute attribute values (R2), drift rates (R3), conflict (R4), and noise (R5). (4) Run evidence accumulation race (R6) over up to T_max deliberation steps. (5) Return the winning action. After execution: (6) Update hunger (R8). (7) Update attribute weights via RPE (R7). Key difference from Formulation 1: action selection is via competitive stochastic accumulation, not softmax. Attribute conflict explicitly increases noise and decision stochasticity.
pseudocode: # R2: Compute attribute values for each action
food_at_pos = [f for f in food_list if f['x'] == pos[0] and f['y'] == pos[1]]
drift = {a: w_imm * av[0] + w_abs * av[1] for a, av in attr_values.items()}
conflict = {a: abs(av[0] - av[1]) for a, av in attr_values.items()}
noise = {a: sigma_0 * (1 + kappa * conflict[a]) for a in actions}
if E[a] >= theta and selected is None:
store drift[selected], attr_values[selected] for update phase

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception['resources']['food']  # list of {'x': int, 'y': int, 'type': 'food', 'palatability': float}
perception_to_variables.food_palatability: resource['palatability'] for each resource in food_sources

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action -> ConsumeEffect reward (1.0 on successful consumption)

expected_behaviors.description: When hungry, agent biases toward closer food (higher w_imm -> higher drift for actions leading to nearby food).
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set H_t = 0.9, two foods: near(dist=1) and far(dist=5, higher palatability) -> assert agent selects move-toward-near more often over 100 trials

expected_behaviors.description: High attribute conflict produces more variable (stochastic) choices. When a_imm and a_abs disagree strongly, noise is amplified.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set food with palatability=0.1 (low a_abs, high a_imm if close) -> measure choice entropy over 100 trials -> assert higher entropy than food with palatability=0.9

expected_behaviors.description: Agent eats food when on the same cell, as eat action gets maximal drift rate (a_imm=1.0, positive a_abs).
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent on food with palatability=0.5, H_t=0.5 -> assert eat action selected > 50% of trials over 100 runs

expected_behaviors.description: With zero conflict (a_imm ~ a_abs), decisions are faster (threshold reached in fewer deliberation steps) and more deterministic.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: record number of deliberation steps to threshold across many decisions -> assert mean steps lower when conflict < 0.2 vs conflict > 1.0

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger rises steadily without eating, causing progressive shift to proximity-seeking behavior.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 100 steps without food -> assert H_t > 0.95 and w_imm > 0.7

references: Rangel, A. (2013). Regulation of dietary choice by the decision-making circuitry. Nature Neuroscience, 16(12), 1717-1724. DOI: 10.1038/nn.3561
Rangel, A., Camerer, C., & Montague, P. R. (2008). A framework for studying the neurobiology of value-based decision making. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 9(7), 545-556. DOI: 10.1038/nrn2357
